Kyle Parker Cunningham
Oeuvre / Painting / 2015

Butterfly Conundrum

Oil on gesso panel · 5.75 × 11.75 inches · 2015, reworked 2020 · CR P.2015.012

This panel began as a study of vertical rhythm — the tall, narrow format borrowed from hanging-scroll painting, the image stacked rather than composed. The butterflies arrived later, as a problem rather than a subject: how does a creature that moves erratically occupy a structure that insists on order?

From the studio logThe original 2015 session notes list three failed underpaintings before the final gesso ground was laid.

The first version sat in the studio for five years. It read as finished from across the room and unfinished from two feet away — a distinction that eventually became impossible to ignore. In the summer of 2020 the panel came back to the easel, and over several weeks a substantial layer of detail went in: the wing structures tightened, the field behind them deepened, and the painting finally agreed to be looked at closely.

A painting is finished when it can survive proximity.

A year after the rework, the image migrated media. The 2021 drypoint isn't a reproduction — the translation from brush to needle forced every decision to be re-made. Where the painting holds its forms in fields of color, the print holds them in accumulated line, and the conundrum of the title shifts from color to mark.
